note: Stunning city of mausoleums (Eva Peron's tomb); the MAIN entrance isn't accessible but there's a separate accessible entrance, and inside the walkways are paved and wide - easy to maneuver. A must-see.

The iconic pink presidential palace on the flat historic Plaza de Mayo (Cathedral, Cabildo nearby); great to see from the square even without a tour, and accessible guided tours (EN/ES) are available. Central and easy.

Buenos Aires' most colourful, photogenic barrio with tango on the street; wheelchair routes lead along Caminito and street souvenirs/outdoor dining are accessible, but most shops have a step to enter. Vibrant, hit-or-miss access. Stay in the tourist core for safety.
One of the world's great opera houses; fully wheelchair accessible with elevators, guided tours daily, and FREE entry for disabled visitors + a companion. A superb, easy accessible highlight.
Large green parks with lakes, rose garden and wide flat paths in the trendy Palermo district (boutiques, cafes, street art nearby); relaxed and stroller/wheelchair-friendly. Great family day.
Modern regenerated waterfront district with wide flat promenades, the Puente de la Mujer footbridge and dining; smooth and fully accessible strolling. Easy and pleasant.
The oldest, most atmospheric barrio with its Sunday antiques market and Plaza Dorrego; charming but cobblestoned and uneven, so bumpy for wheels. The market is lively but tight.
Modern art museum in Palermo; fully wheelchair accessible with lifts - a reliable accessible cultural stop and good rainy-day option.
On Plaza de Mayo (where Pope Francis served as Archbishop); wheelchair accessible main floor. Combine with Casa Rosada and the Cabildo for an accessible historic-centre loop.
A famous bookshop in a converted grand theatre; the stunning ground floor is accessible and reachable, though the upper balcony levels have stairs. A quick, beautiful stop.
